coredata.dc:title: pH controlled green luminescent carbon dots derived from benzoxazine monomers for the fluorescence turn-on and turn-off detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Bin
Lu, Xingchang
Hu, Junyi
Zhang, Geng
Zheng, Xinsheng
He, Limin
Cao, Jianbo
Gu, Jiangjiang
Cao, Feifei
coredata.dc:description: Emerging carbon dots (CDs) are widely used as fluorescent probes in biological and environmental fields, nevertheless, the control of CDs based on different detection mechanisms is rarely reported. In this paper, green luminescent CDs (G-CDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment of benzoxazine monomers (BZM). The obtained G-CDs showed pH dependent photoluminescence, which could be designed as fluorescence turn-on and turn-off sensors. The G-CDs exhibited weak photoluminescence at pH = 7.0 and could be turned on by Zn(II) selectively with the limitation of 0.32 μM in the concentration range from 1 to 100 μM. When pH = 10.0, Cr(VI) could quench the strong fluorescence of G-CDs efficiently, and the limit of detection was 0.99 μM with a linear range of 1–50 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence turn-on and turn-off performance of G-CDs was attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) of Zn(II) and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI), respectively. The excellent probes were successfully applied for the detection of Zn(II) in biological system and Cr(VI) in environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green luminescent carbon dots
Benzoxazine monomers
Fluorescent sensor
Zn(II)
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Zn(II) and Cr(VI)
Synthetic strategy and characterization of G-CDs
G-CDs for Zn(II) detection at pH 7.0
G-CDs for Cr(VI) detection at pH 10.0

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2662016QD028 , 2662015PY163 , 2662017JC025 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603080 ), the Open Funds of The State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology ( AMLKF201702 ) and Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS, BNLMS20160102 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis procedure of BZM and G-CDs.
(a) TEM image of G-CDs (inset: HRTEM image of an individual G-CD); (b) size distribution of G-CDs; (c) AFM image of G-CDs; (d) the height profile along the line.
(a) FT-IR spectra comparison between BZM and G-CDs; high resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), O1s (d) XPS spectra of G-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of G-CDs dispersed in water, with inset photographs showing the G-CDs aqueous solution: under day light (left) and UV irradiation (right); (b) excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of the G-CDs in water with normalized curves (inset).
The effect of pH (a), temperature (b), ions strength (c) and time under ultraviolet light irradiation (d) on G-CDs. The error bars represented the standard deviation of three measurements.

(a) Photographs of G-CDs with different metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation in neutral conditions. (b) Relative fluorescence intensity change (λex = 380 nm) of aqueous G-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) in the presence of 200 μM of various metal ions in pH = 7.0. (c) The plot of the fluorescence intensity against Zn(II) concentration within the range of 0–500 μM, inset showed linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Zn(II) concentration. I0 represented the fluorescence intensity without Zn(II), and I represented the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs with various ions (b) or different concentration of Zn(II) (c) (λex = 380 nm; λem = 460 nm). The error bars represented the standard deviation of three measurements.

(a) Photographs of G-CDs with different anions under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation in alkaline conditions. (b) Normalized fluorescence intensity of aqueous G-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) in the presence of 200 μM of various anions in pH = 10.0. (c) Plot of the fluorescence intensity against Cr(VI) concentration within the range of 0–300 μM. Inset: linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Cr(VI) concentration. I0 represented the fluorescence intensity without Cr(VI), and I represented the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs with various ions (b) or different concentrations of Cr(VI) (c) (λex = 380 nm; λem = 460 nm). The error bars represented the standard deviation of three measurements.
G-CDs for the real sample detection.

head.ce:title: pH controlled green luminescent carbon dots derived from benzoxazine monomers for the fluorescence turn-on and turn-off detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Public Laboratory of Electron Microscopy, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: B. Fang and X. Lu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging carbon dots (CDs) are widely used as fluorescent probes in biological and environmental fields, nevertheless, the control of CDs based on different detection mechanisms is rarely reported. In this paper, green luminescent CDs (G-CDs) were prepared by a facile hydrothermal treatment of benzoxazine monomers (BZM). The obtained G-CDs showed pH dependent photoluminescence, which could be designed as fluorescence turn-on and turn-off sensors. The G-CDs exhibited weak photoluminescence at pH = 7.0 and could be turned on by Zn(II) selectively with the limitation of 0.32 μM in the concentration range from 1 to 100 μM. When pH = 10.0, Cr(VI) could quench the strong fluorescence of G-CDs efficiently, and the limit of detection was 0.99 μM with a linear range of 1–50 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence turn-on and turn-off performance of G-CDs was attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) of Zn(II) and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI), respectively. The excellent probes were successfully applied for the detection of Zn(II) in biological system and Cr(VI) in environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn a great attention as the promising carbon based photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterials since they were discovered in 2004 and defined in 2006 [1–3]. As the novel zero-dimension nanomaterials, CDs possess many excellent properties, including outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, high aqueous solubility and high photostability [4–8]. As a consequence of these remarkable advantages, CDs have a variety of applications in bioimaging [9–11], photovoltaic devices [12,13] photocatalysis [14,15] and sensing [16–20]. CDs for sensing underwent rapid development in the past decade [6,7,21], and a series of novel CDs were developed for the detection of various substances including cations, anions and biomolecules [17,19,22–28]. Most of these fluorescent sensing systems were established on the base of fluorescence quenching or fluorescence recovery. On the other hand, CDs for the fluorescence turn-on detection were explored in very recent years [29,30]. These detection probes based on fluorescence enhancement response exhibit sensitive colour change and good anti-interference performance. However, there is no report about a kind of CDs that simultaneously possess both fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence quenching properties. Moreover, the control of these two properties of CDs is still an interesting challenge up to now, which also has a remarkable significance for the multi-functional sensing applications of CDs.

As most reports believe, the structure of CDs usually contains carbogenic cores and passivated functional groups on their surface [7,31]. It is reported that these functional groups play an important role for the control of unique physical properties of CDs [6,10,32]. For example, Zheng et al. enhanced the quantum yield of CDs from 2 to 24% by oxidating the functional groups on the surface of CDs [33]. Our group was focus on the fabrication of novel multi-functional CDs [22] and found the solubility of CDs was depended on the surface functional groups [34]. Furthermore, the yields of CDs with different solubilities could be regulated by means of the design of the initial waterborne polyurethane emulsion. Therefore, it is likely to tune the functional groups of CDs though selecting the suitable carbon source, which will result in the control of fluorescence turn-on and turn-off properties of CDs.

Benzoxazine monomers (BZM) are the precursor of polybenzoxazine resins, which demonstrate high thermal stability. BZM are easily synthesized from various phenols, primary amines and formaldehyde, which provides the flexibility of molecular structures [35]. Inspired by the high nitrogen content in BZM, they are expected to be the excellent raw material to prepare CDs with more nitrogen functional groups and high quantum yield.

In this work, we successfully fabricated highly green luminescent CDs (G-CDs) from BZM through a facile hydrothermal method. The as-prepared G-CDs exhibited excellent dispersibility in water and pH depended PL intensity, which could be designed for the fluorescence turn-on and turn-off detection towards Zn(II) and Cr(VI), respectively. In neutral conditions (pH = 7.0), the G-CDs showed weak PL intensity and the fluorescence turn-on procedure was achieved selectively and sensitively by adding Zn(II). In alkaline conditions (pH = 10.0), the strong fluorescence of G-CDs was quenched efficiently with the addition of Cr(VI). In addition, the pH controlled fluorescence turn-on and turn-off detection of G-CDs were caused by the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) of Zn(II) and the inner filter effect (IFE) of Cr(VI), respectively. This platform was successfully used for the detection of Zn(II) in serum and Cr(VI) in lake water.

Melamine, phenol (Ph), formaldehyde solution (37–40 wt%), methanol, HCl, NaOH, FeCl3, CoCl2, Cu(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, AlCl3, CrCl3, MnCl2, PbCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2, CdCl2, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, KF, KBr, KI, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, K2S2O8, K2CO3, KNO3, NaNO2, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, NaClO and sodium acetate (NaAc) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fluorescein was purchased from Adamas-beta Inc. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without any further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was obtained from a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q) and used throughout all the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was observed on a Hitachi H-7650 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was obtained by a Talos F200C electron microscope (FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was captured in tapping mode on a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded by using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, USA) with a filtered Cu Ka radiation. The Raman spectrum was obtained on a Lab RAM Aramis spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were conducted on a NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by the ESCALab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific). 1H NMR spectrum was collected on a Bruker AVANCE AV500 spectrometer using D2O as solvent. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was recorded between 200 and 700 nm on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay was conducted on a TimePro-01 lifetime fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, France).

In a typical synthesis of BZM, 6.4 g melamine, 4.5 mL phenol, 25 mL of aqueous formaldehyde (37–40 wt%) were introduced into a solvent of 100 mL methanol with vigorous stirring in a 250 mL round-bottom flask at 80 °C for six hours. Afterwards, a decompressing distillation was used to remove the solvent of methanol from the reaction mixture at 55 °C. Then the mixture was dissolved in chloroform and washed by sodium hydroxide aqueous solution to remove the unreacted phenol. After removing the chloroform, the obtained liquid was of low-viscosity, transparent, and colorless, indicating that BZ was successfully synthesized.

G-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.3 g BZM and 30 mL 0.5 M NaOH aqueous solution were mixed and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the obtained brownish yellow solution was neutralized by the addition of HCl and was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to precipitate out particle aggregates. The supernatant containing G-CDs was further purified with a silica column chromatography using mixture of ethyl acetate and methanol as the eluent. Finally, the product was freeze-dried to obtain the pure product with the yield of 75.9 mg for further use. For comparison, melamine (155 mg), phenol (108 μL) and formaldehyde (200 μL) were added into 30 mL 0.5 M NaOH aqueous solution and suffered the same preparation steps and purification process of G-CDs, which was finally obtained as blue luminescent CDs (B-CDs).

The QY of the as-prepared G-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
Q
Y sam
= Q
Y ref
I sam
A ref
n
sam
2
I ref
A sam
n
ref
2
where “sam” and “ref” refer to sample and reference, respectively. “I” is the integrated emission intensity, “A” represents UV–vis absorbance at 410 nm, and “n” is the refractive index (1.33) of the solutions. The QY of fluorescein is reported to be 0.95 when dissolved in 0.1 M NaOH in the literature [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and quantitative analysis for Zn(II) and Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Zn(II) and Cr(VI) were explored in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) and Borax-NaOH buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 10.0) at room temperature, respectively. For the detection of Zn(II), typically, the G-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) and ZnCl2 solution were added into Tris-HCl buffer solution and the final concentration of Zn(II) was in the range of 0–500 μM. After 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were recorded at 380 nm excitation. For comparison, the same procedure was performed for other metal ions at the final concentration of 200 μM. To detect Cr(VI), the G-CDs (0.25 mg/mL) and K2Cr2O7 solution were added into Borax-NaOH buffer solution and the final concentration of Cr(VI) was in the range of 0–300 μM. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were recorded at 380 nm excitation. Then other anions were added in the same way at the final concentration of 200 μM.

The serum sample was obtained by a commercial way and the lake water was collected from the south lake (Wuhan, China). These samples were filtered with 0.22 μM membranes to remove the large suspended particles. Their pH levels were regulated to 7.0 and 10.0, respectively. Then 2.4 mL of sample, 300 μL of Zn(II) or Cr(VI) solution and 300 μL of G-CDs (2.5 mg/mL) were mixed and recorded with the fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic strategy and characterization of G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: BZM is a kind of resin precursor with the oxazine ring structure that is attached to a benzene ring, and it possesses high designability by changing the phenol and amine sources. In the present work, we synthesized BZM from melamine, phenol, and formaldehyde by one-step Mannich reaction according to our previous work [37,38], which was described in Fig. 1 . As the BZM is insoluble in water, the conversion of BZM into water-dispersible CDs was a critical step. Here, we utilized a one-pot hydrothermal method to produce excellent G-CDs at 200 °C for 6 h in NaOH aqueous solution. Under the high temperature and great pressure, chain segments of BZM suffered scission, then formed G-CDs by a carbonization procedure. As a consequence of the nitrogen content of 34%, BZM was not only as carbon source, but also acted as nitrogen source for the N self-doping process to develop G-CDs with more nitrogen functional groups. It demonstrated that this method based on carbonization procedure was an efficient and rapid way for the preparation of G-CDs. In addition, B-CDs were also prepared from the raw materials of BZM (melamine, phenol and formaldehyde) for comparison.

The morphology and size distribution of G-CDs were characterized by using TEM, HRTEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the as-prepared G-CDs were uniformly spherical nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm and well dispersed. The Fig. 2b exhibited the narrow size distribution of G-CDs with 4.0–5.8 nm in diameter, and the average diameter was calculated as 4.6 nm (obtained by counting approximately 50 G-CDs). The HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 2a) showed the lattice spacing as 0.23 nm, which referred to the (1 0 0) fact of graphitic structure [39]. As demonstrated in Fig. 2c and d, the AFM results further proved that the G-CDs were mostly spherical in shape and with sub-10 nm height. Fig. S1 showed the XRD pattern of G-CDs. The peak at 25.7° was corresponding to the interlayer distance (0.35 nm), which was attributed to the graphite crystalline of the CDs [40]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S2) of G-CDs exhibited two peaks at 1385 cm−1 (D band) and 1591 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbon structure respectively [41].

G-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS to identify their surface composition and chemical state. Fig. 3 a demonstrated the FT-IR spectra of BZM and G-CDs. For BZM, the peak at 1177 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CNC. The peak at 1070 cm−1 corresponded to the COC stretching vibration. Therefore, the FT-IR results illustrated the emergence of oxazine ring, which confirmed that the BZM was successfully obtained. Compared with BZM, several distinct characteristic peaks were observed for G-CDs. The peaks occurred at 3340 cm−1 and 3175 cm−1, which were corresponded to the NH/OH. The stronger peak of 1670 cm−1 was ascribed to the vibration absorption of CO in COOH, and the peak at 1410 cm−1 corresponded to COO− group [42]. Another peak at 1240 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CN/CN [43]. These results indicated the G-CDs possessed desirable carboxylate and amide groups. Fig. S3 depicted the element identification of G-CDs by XPS, which showed that C: N: O was 63.62%: 7.29%: 29.09% in G-CDs. Oher obvious peaks centered at 63.5, 198.5 and 497.0 were assigned to Na2s, Cl2p and Na Auger, which was resulted from the absorption effect of Na and Cl ions. These excess Na and Cl ions could react with COO− and CONH2 functional groups, respectively, to improve the solubility of G-CDs in water. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) could be deconvoluted into six peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.7, 286.5, 287.8, 288.7 eV, which demonstrated the existence of CC, CC, CN, CO, OCN and OCO bands, respectively [43,44]. The high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 3c) had three peaks at 399.0, 399.7, 401.3 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and NH/NH2 groups [42,45]. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 3d) exhibited two peaks at 531.7 and 532.6 eV, which were associated with OCN and COH/COC bands, respectively [21,44]. Thus, the FT-IR and XPS analysis results provided convincing evidence for the surface functional groups of G-CDs, indicating the formation of carboxylate and amide groups on the surface of G-CDs. 1H NMR spectrum was measured to investigate the detail structure of G-CDs and the result was demonstrated in Fig. S4. There were three kinds of protons with different chemical environments: 1–3 ppm for sp3 CH protons, 3–5 ppm for the protons attached with hydroxyl, carbonyl and amide groups (4.89 ppm for the solvent peak), and >8 ppm for the aromatic protons [46].

To explore the optical properties of the as-obtained G-CDs, their UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were studied at room temperature. As shown in the Fig. 4 a inset, the diluted aqueous solution of the as-prepared G-CDs was transparent and colorless in daylight, and it emitted green fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. Fig. 4a showed the UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs. It was observed that two characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks were located at 270 nm and 330 nm. The single peak at 270 nm was typically corresponding to the π–π* transition of the conjugated CC bond from the carbon core, leading to weak observed fluorescence emission [24,32]. The broad absorption band centered at 330 nm was ascribed to the n–π* transition of the CO bond and the contribution of surface/molecular section [47,48]. Also as shown in Fig. 4a, the emission spectrum of the G-CDs with an excitation of 380 nm was demonstrated and it could be seen that the optimal emission wavelength was located at 500 nm.

The PL emission spectra with various excitation wavelengths were demonstrated in Fig. 4b. The wavelength of the maximum emission of the G-CDs shifted from 485 to 513 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 350 to 420 nm, indicating that the emission of G-CDs possessed excitation-dependent features. The excitation-dependent PL behavior were similar to the common carbon dots, which was caused by the complex carbon cores or surface defects [47,49–51]. The B-CDs derived from melamine, phenol and formaldehyde were also with less than 10 nm size (Fig. S5a), however their PL spectra showed blue emission under 340–400 nm excitation (Fig. S5b). The emission peak was located at 460 nm when the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, which was different from that of G-CDs. The QY of fluorescence for the as-prepared G-CDs was calculated to be about 18.0% (Table S1) by using fluorescein as a reference.

To investigate the fluorescence responses of the G-CDs to various pH values, the emission intensity of G-CDs in solutions with different pH values (range from 7 to 14) were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 5 a. At the pH range from 7 to 9, the PL intensity significantly increased as pH value increased. It was apparent that the PL intensity was minimum at pH 7. Then in the pH range of 9–12, the PL intensity had no significant change. The PL intensity had a slight decrease as the pH value increased from 12 to 14. To sum up, the resultant G-CDs showed weak PL activity in the neutral condition and exhibited strong PL intensity in the basic condition, indicating that G-CDs might have potential applications for the detection based on fluorescence turn-on and turn-off effects.

The effect of temperature on G-CDs was also investigated and showed in Fig. 5b. The PL intensity of G-CDs remained stable in the range from 10 to 60 °C. The change of ion strength did not cause obvious decrease of PL intensity of G-CDs (Fig. 5c), even at a high concentration of NaCl (1.0 M). The photobleaching property of G-CDs was tested under sustaining ultraviolet light irradiation. Fig. 5d showed the normalized PL intensity declined to 82.6 ± 1.1% after illumination for 5 h, which suggested a good photostability of G-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-CDs for Zn(II) detection at pH 7.0
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc ion is the second most abundant trace metal ion after iron in human body and it plays crucial roles in biological systems such as maintaining key structural features of gene transcription proteins and neural signal transmission [52,53]. Therefore, acquiring a highly selective and sensitive detection probe of Zn(II) is important and imperative. It was inspiringly found that Zn(II) could enhance the weak fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared G-CDs and the feasibility of G-CDs as probes for Zn(II) detection in similar physiology environment (pH = 7.0) was investigated in detail.

To evaluate the selectivity of the present sensing system, we investigated the PL responses of G-CDs to 13 types of cations (200 μM). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the excellent selectivity was visualized by the naked eye using a 365 nm UV lamp. Only Zn(II) obviously induced the fluorescence enhancing in an aqueous solution. From the fluorescence emission spectra change in Fig. S6, it was obvious that the G-CDs had no response to these metal ions including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Mn2+. However, the PL intensity of G-CDs showed an evident increase after the addition of Zn(II). It also could be seen more clearly from the calculated ratios of (I − I0)/I0 in Fig. 6b. The (I − I0)/I0 value of G-CDs with Zn(II) was 1.870 ± 0.003, which was much higher than that of our probes treated with other cations. Therefore, it was noticeable that only Zn(II) could induce a serious increase of the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs, implying that the as-prepared G-CDs possessed excellent fluorescence sensing selectivity toward Zn(II). The specificity of G-CDs for Zn(II) detection was due to the affinity of Zn(II) [54]. The pH effect on G-CDs for the detection of Zn(II) was investigated and the result was shown in Fig. S7. It was obvious that the fluorescence enhancement efficiency reached highest at pH = 7, and the fluorescence intensity had little change when pH was higher than 11. Thus the optimal condition for Zn(II) detection was pH = 7. The sensitivity of this Zn(II)-sensing system was further investigated in detail, different concentrations of Zn(II) solution were added to the aqueous solution of G-CDs and the fluorescence emission intensity in 460 nm was measured. As shown in Fig. S8, the PL intensity of G-CDs increased gradually with Zn(II) concentration increasing from 0 to 500 μM, demonstrating that addition of Zn(II) could efficiently enhance the fluorescence of the G-CDs. Fig. 6c presented the relationship between fluorescence intensity and Zn(II) concentration in the range between 0 and 500 μM. The ratios of (I − I0)/I0 had a good linear correlation with Zn(II) concentrations over the range of 0–100 μM with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.997. The linear regression equation was shown as blow:
I
I 0
- 1 = 0.01261 c + 0.03977
where “c” was the Zn(II) concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.32 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N), which was superior to some recent reports (Table S2). In order to investigate whether the chemical oxygen demand (COD) and biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) could affect the detection limitation of Zn(II), natural lake water (COD: 49.0 mg/L, BOD: 21.8 mg/L) was used to explore the linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Zn(II) concentration (Fig. S9). And the LOD for Zn(II) detection was calculated to be 0.50 μM (S/N = 3). The high selectivity and sensitivity of our G-CDs indicated that it could be used to detect Zn(II) quantitatively in the solution at pH 7.0.

To confirm the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of G-CDs towards Zn(II), the FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs+Zn(II) was measured and shown in Fig. S10. The absorption peaks corresponding to CO in and COO− groups shifted to 1591 and 1361 cm−1, respectively, which revealed that the chelation occurred between the carboxyl groups and Zn(II). Moreover, the blue shift emission of G-CDs with the addition of Zn(II) proved that the fluorescence turn-on phenomenon was attributed to ICT [55]. The donor and acceptor parts were supposed as the functional groups on the surface of G-CDs (such as COO−) and the core of G-CDs, respectively. To confirm the importance of COO− groups, G-CDs were dispersed in 0.1 M ammonium hydroxide and the hydrothermal reaction was done at 120 °C for 3 h to prepare CDs with more amide groups (Ami-CDs). As the TEM image (Fig. S11a) showed, the Ami-CDs had size smaller than 10 nm. The peak of CO in amide groups in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S11b) increased and the peak of COO− decreased, which meant that Ami-CDs had more amide groups. However, as Fig. S11c demonstrated, the PL intensity (460 nm, λex = 380 nm) of Ami-CDs increased not very much ((I − I0)/I0 = 0.376 ± 0.019) after the addition of Zn(II), which confirmed the key role of COO− groups for the fluorescence turn-on sensing toward Zn(II). Lifetime fluorescence spectroscopy of G-CDs and G-CDs+Zn(II) were carried out and the results were shown in Fig. S12. By calculation, the fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs and G-CDs+Zn(II) were 3.00 and 6.50 ns, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: G-CDs for Cr(VI) detection at pH 10.0
ce:section.ce:para: Chromium(VI) [Cr(VI)] are regarded as one of the major water pollutants, because of its highly carcinogenic and mutagenic properties [56]. These chromium(VI) pollutants are produced almost by modern industries, including electroplating, leather tanning and wood preservation, and generally dispersed in alkaline industrial waste water [21,57]. Therefore, the selective and sensitive determination for Cr (VI) in basic conditions is of great significance for avoiding it into groundwater even drinking water. In this paper, the resultant G-CDs showed a strong fluorescence intensity in alkaline conditions and could be quenched by Cr(VI). Hence, the anion sensing capacity of the as-obtained G-CDs was evaluated detailedly at pH 10.0.

The fluorescence response of the G-CDs to 14 types of anions (200 μM) was investigated for evaluating the selectivity of this sensing system. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the G-CDs solution emitted strong green color with no anion added under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. After the addition of Cr(VI), the fluorescence of G-CDs was quenched almost completely, while no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed in the presence of other anions. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence emission spectra changes of G-CDs indicated that Cr(VI) could quench the fluorescence of G-CDs evidently. Then the normalized intensity (I/I0) of these fluorescence emission spectra were exhibited in Fig. 7b detailedly and numerically illustrated that this sensing system had almost no response to most of the anions, such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2O8 2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, CH3COO−, ClO−, and CO3 2−. After the addition of 200 μM Cr(VI), the fluorescence of G-CDs was quenched immediately. It is a pity that the MnO4 − was found capable of quenching G-CDs to its 70% of PL intensity, which was likely to be attributed to the highly oxidability of the MnO4 −. Here I and I0 represented the fluorescent intensities of G-CDs at 500 nm in the presence and absence of anions, respectively. These results clearly demonstrated that the G-CDs fluorescent sensing system was potential and selective toward Cr(VI) over other anions. The pH effect on G-CDs for the detection of Cr(VI) was investigated and the result was shown in Fig. S14. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was low at pH = 7 or 8 and became higher when pH was 9. The higher pH did not have obvious effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency, which resulted in the suitable condition for Cr(VI) detection. In addition to the selectivity of G-CDs towards Cr(VI), their sensitivity was also investigated in detail. With addition of Cr(VI) from 1 to 300 μM, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was gradually quenched (Fig. S15). As shown in Fig. 7c, the relationship between fluorescence response and Cr(VI) concentration was indicated. The ratio of I0/I had a good linear relationship with Cr(VI) concentration over the range of 1–50 μM and with a correlation coefficient square (R2) of 0.995 (Fig. 7c inset). Here I0 and I represented PL intensity of G-CDs at 500 nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. The linear regression equation was exhibited blow:
I 0
I
= 0.013 c + 0.989
where “c” was the concentration of Cr(VI). Furthermore, the quenching efficiency was fitted by a typical Stern-Volmer equation: I0/I = 1 + KSVQ, where KSV was the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and Q was the concentration of Cr(VI) [58]. The value of Stern–Volmer quenching constant KSV was determined to be 1.3 × 104 M−1. Then, the LOD was calculated to be 0.99 μM (S/N = 3), which was compared or superior to the previous reports (Table S3). The linear relationship between I0/I and Cr(VI) concentration (Fig. S16) in natural lake water was also investigated and the LOD for Cr(VI) detection was calculated to be 2.05 μM (S/N = 3). Based on these results, it was feasible for G-CD sensing system to detect Cr(VI) selectively and sensitively in a alkaline condition.

To confirm the mechanism of the turn-off response of G-CDs for Cr(VI), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the maximum fluorescence excitation of the G-CDs at pH = 10.0 were measured. As shown in Fig. S17a, the strong absorption band of Cr(VI) can overlap well with the excitation band of G-CDs, which cause the IFE [59,60]. When the excitation light reached the solution contained G-CDs and Cr(VI), Cr(VI) could absorb most of the light and the G-CDs could not get enough light to excite the green fluorescence. To confirm the specificity of G-CDs for Cr(VI) detection, the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S17b) of various ions were explored and the results revealed that only Cr(VI) had the strong absorption around 380 nm, while other ions could not result in IFE.

To further investigate the IFE mechanism, corrected fluorescence intensities could be calculated according to the following equation [61]:
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
wherein, “Fobsd” is the measured fluorescence intensity and “Fcor” represents the corrected fluorescence intensity by removing IFE from Fobsd. “Aex” and “Aem” stand for the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 380 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λem = 500 nm), respectively. “s” is the thickness of excitation beam (0.10 cm), “g” is the fixed distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm) and “d” is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). The absorbance and fluorescence intensities of G-CDs with the addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) were summarized in Table S4. Corrected factor (CF) is calculated according to Fcor/Fobsd, and Fcor,0 is the corrected fluorescence intensity of G-CDs in absence of Cr(VI). The suppressed efficiency (E) is calculated according to 1 − F/F0 and showed in Fig. S18. It showed that approximately 80% of the suppressed efficiency originated from the IFE of Cr(VI) [62]. The fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs+Cr(VI) was calculated to be 2.74 ns in Fig. S12, which was almost the same with that of G-CDs.

To investigate the effect of real samples on the detection of Zn (II) and Cr (VI) based on G-CDs, serum and lake water were tested and the results were shown in Table 1 . When different concentration of Zn(II) was added into the serum sample with G-CDs at pH = 7.0, the recovery efficiencies were in the range of 97.2–106.5% and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.08–5.33%. When 5 and 20 μM Cr(VI) were added into the later water sample with G-CDs at pH = 10.0, the recovery efficiencies were 96.6 and 95.9% and the corresponding RSDs were 5.03 and 3.14%. These results revealed that the G-CDs could be used for the determination of Zn(II) and Cr(VI) in some practical samples.

In summary, we developed a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize monodisperse highly green luminescent carbon dots (G-CDs) with QY (18.0%) by the carbonization of the benzoxazine monomers in NaOH aqueous solution. Compared with previous reports [17,19,22–30], these resultant G-CDs demonstrated a novel property of pH controlled fluorescence turn-on and turn-off detection. When pH = 7.0, the weak PL intensity of G-CDs could be increased by addition of Zn(II) with high selectivity and sensitivity (LOD: 0.32 μM, linear range: 1–100 μM). While in alkaline conditions (here employed pH = 10.0), the fluorescence turn-off detection procedure was achieved because the strong PL intensity of G-CDs could be selectively and sensitively quenched with addition of Cr(VI) (LOD: 0.99 μM, linear range: 1–50 μM). Furthermore, this performance was attributed to the ICT of Zn(II) and IFE of Cr(VI). In neural conditions, the carboxylate groups on the surface of G-CDs interacted with Zn(II) and caused fluorescence increasing. Cr(VI) could absorb the excitation light and resulted in the fluorescence quenching in alkaline conditions. This excellent fluorescence turn-on and turn-off sensor could be useful for the detection of Zn(II) in serum sample and Cr(VI) in lake water with satisfactory results, indicating the potential of G-CDs for multi-functional sensing in practical systems. We believe this work will inspire the design of other fluorescent sensors based on CDs, and further effort is needed to develop red or near-infrared luminescent CDs for in vivo applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2662016QD028, 2662015PY163, 2662017JC025), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603080), the Open Funds of The State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology (AMLKF201702) and Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS, BNLMS20160102).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.10.088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
